title: Exploring Borrowed Words and Sentence Variety
simple_explanation: This page teaches us that English words come from all over the world! It also shows us how writers use short sentences to make things exciting and long sentences to add lots of detail. We learn to use special words like 'like' or 'as' to make fun comparisons, and linking words to make our stories smooth.
detailed_explanation: Writers use different sentence lengths to make their stories more interesting. Long sentences can describe many actions happening at once, making the story feel fast or detailed. Short sentences can create a sudden impact or quicken the pace, making readers feel excited or surprised. Using 'similes' (comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as') and 'repetition' (saying something again) can make your writing more powerful and memorable. Connecting words, called 'connectives' (like 'and', 'but', 'since', 'after'), are super important! They help link your ideas and make your story flow smoothly.

examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of science fiction stories in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Long sentence example: 'The taller boy pushed Gavin back to the ground, snatched the sports bag and ran off, while his accomplice threw the jinn into the road and followed him.' (Many actions in one sentence).
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of science fiction stories in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Short sentence example: 'They didn't get far.' (Quick and to the point).
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of science fiction stories in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Simile example: 'Eager's arms shot out like giant snakes.' (Compares arms to snakes).
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of science fiction stories in a real sentence.

examples_expanded.text: Connectives: 'I want to play *and* read, *but* I need to finish my homework *before* I can.'
examples_expanded.context: This example shows how we use the concept of science fiction stories in a real sentence.

guided_practice: Identify the meaning of 'simile' in a sentence. Remember it means: a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
Match the word 'repetition' with a similar concept from your own experience.
Look at the first example again. How would you modify it to make it your own?
application_tasks: Create 3 of your own sentences using the core idea of Science fiction stories.
Imagine you are teaching this topic to a friend. Write down exactly what you would say.
challenge_tasks: Combine the concept of 'simile' with something you learned in a previous chapter to write a short paragraph.
Can you find an exception to this rule or a creative way to bend it? Explain how.
key_points: English has many words borrowed from other languages.
Varying sentence length (long and short) helps control the pace and detail of your story.
Use similes to make descriptions more imaginative.
Repetition can add emphasis and impact to your writing.
Connectives are essential for linking ideas and making your story flow well.
Proofread your work carefully to check punctuation, especially commas.

vocabulary.definition: a figure of speech comparing two different things using 'like' or 'as'
the action of repeating something that has already been said or written for emphasis
words or phrases that link ideas or sentences together (e.g., and, but, since)
